coredata.dc:title: A study on the photophysical properties of strong green-fluorescent N-doped carbon dots and application for pH sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav, Rajnee
Vikas,
Lahariya, Vikas
Tanwar, Manushree
Kumar, Rajesh
Das, Anirban
Sadhana, K.
coredata.dc:description: Color tuneable effective pH sensor in both acidic and alkaline medium is important for environment and bioanalytical purpose. In this report, natural biomass derived N-doped fluorescent carbon dots are utilized as pH sensor. The fast microwave irradiation method was employed for synthesis using natural sweet lime juice extract. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was used for nitrogen doping with different amounts. The prepared N-doped carbon dots are comprehensively studied by various characterization methods to analyse the effect of dopant concentrations on photophysical and luminescence properties. Improved thermal stability from 900 °C to 1000 °C with EDA content is observed. The fluorescence spectra of prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibited stable green emission with high quantum yield 83 % under ultraviolet and 440 nm irradiation wavelengths for an optimal concentration of EDA. The color of emission confirmed by CIE plot and about 3.47 ns decay time is found by PL decay curve. The anti-photo bleaching ability and good photostability for 10 days are found. The prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibit high ionic stability and bio compatibility. Further, pH dependent color tuneable (green to yellow) emission spectra of prepared N-doped carbon dots displayed high fluorescence in acidic medium at pH -5. Although, emission was quenched and red shifted in alkaline medium. The effective pH-sensitivity, and good reversibility of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots samples were tested with real water samples having different pH values. The reasonable mechanism is discussed. It depicts the prepared N-doped carbon dots have potential to serve as pH sensor for physiochemical and bioanalytical measurements.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Color tuneable effective pH sensor in both acidic and alkaline medium is important for environment and bioanalytical purpose. In this report, natural biomass derived N-doped fluorescent carbon dots are utilized as pH sensor. The fast microwave irradiation method was employed for synthesis using natural sweet lime juice extract. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was used for nitrogen doping with different amounts. The prepared N-doped carbon dots are comprehensively studied by various characterization methods to analyse the effect of dopant concentrations on photophysical and luminescence properties. Improved thermal stability from 900 °C to 1000 °C with EDA content is observed. The fluorescence spectra of prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibited stable green emission with high quantum yield 83 % under ultraviolet and 440 nm irradiation wavelengths for an optimal concentration of EDA. The color of emission confirmed by CIE plot and about 3.47 ns decay time is found by PL decay curve. The anti-photo bleaching ability and good photostability for 10 days are found. The prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibit high ionic stability and bio compatibility. Further, pH dependent color tuneable (green to yellow) emission spectra of prepared N-doped carbon dots displayed high fluorescence in acidic medium at pH -5. Although, emission was quenched and red shifted in alkaline medium. The effective pH-sensitivity, and good reversibility of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots samples were tested with real water samples having different pH values. The reasonable mechanism is discussed. It depicts the prepared N-doped carbon dots have potential to serve as pH sensor for physiochemical and bioanalytical measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: An emerging category of nanoscale carbon-based nanomaterials with acing photoluminescence capabilities known as “carbon dots” has drawn enormous attention worldwide from researchers and technologists. Carbon dots are superior because of their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high stability, and strong resistance ability to photodegradation than the conventional quantum dots and natural dyes. Given these benefits, carbon dots are the appropriate alternatives to hazardous metallic elements-based traditional semiconductor quantum dots for various environmental, bio analytical, and bio medical applications. Recently, carbon dots as optical sensors, biosensors, chemical sensors, fluorescent sensor, and pH sensor are most in demand. pH sensor is the foremost tool for analytical research in food, environment, and bio-based applications. pH is one of the thermodynamic variables which has significant impact on biochemical and physiological processes [1]. The precise quantification of pH is of great importance due to its extensive applications in medical, biological samples, drinkable and eatable items, and environmental safety. Earlier, pH-sensitive probes based on organic dyes and quantum dots have been fabricated to defeat the shortcomings including photobleaching, phototoxicity, and autofluorescence of conventional organic fluorescent dye [2]. Numerous fluorescent nanomaterials have been applied for pH detection, including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), organic dyes, and fluorescent polymers [3]. However, low sensitivity and high toxicity due to the use of heavy metals in synthesis and poor chemical and photo stability restrict their uses. Thus, to find real time access, long life span and biocompatible pH sensor is still awaited. It can be achieved by using carbon dots as probe due to their smaller size, good photostability and bio compatibility. Moreover, exceptional fluorescent and non- toxic behavior of carbon dots are favorable for pH monitoring systems in environmental, pharmaceutical and vitro/in-vivo biological applications. Recently, fluorescent carbon nanomaterials including graphene quantum dots and carbon dots have been developed for effective pH sensing [4,5]. The advantage of water solubility, low photo bleaching and tunable emission offer carbon dots as promising candidate for fluorescence sensors and other applications such as photovoltaic, biosensor, catalysis, and imaging.

Novel carbon dots are advanced fluorescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with size-dependent excellent optoelectronic properties. Carbon dots are spherical and have either amorphous or graphitic core depending on the synthesis temperature. High-temperature synthesis processes resulted in a graphitic core while carbon dots prepared at low temperature are found to have an amorphous core [6]. In addition, carbon dots surface is rich with functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, ether, and carboxyl which help them to dissolve in solvents easily and contribute to their tunable fluorescent properties. Carbon dots were first discovered due to their fluorescence behavior [7]. Since then, rapid efforts have been proceeding with this nanomaterial due to its wide applicability in energy, environment, and bio- imaging applications etc. In literature, the luminescent phenomenon of carbon dots is still controversial and not clearly explained by a single mechanism. According to the information found in published sources, the luminescence observed in carbon dots might arise from (i) the quantum confinement effect, (ii) functional groups on their surface, and (iii) transitions between molecular states. The functional groups developed on its surface and their interaction with carbon core construct the absorbance and emission characteristics of carbon dots. One of the popular methods to achieve high fluorescence is by modifying the surface through passivation or doping with non-metals like nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, sulfur, and oxygen etc. elements. In doped carbon dots, the doping atom can alter the electronic structure of carbon dots as well as modify the optical characteristics. The dopant ions offer their energy state in the bandgap region either as donor or acceptor, thereby radiative transition from these states emit the tunable emission in visible region. Among all above mentioned dopant ions, nitrogen with equivalent in size and more electronegative to carbon, is considered as perfect dopant. It has five valence electrons, from which 4 electrons strongly bonded with carbon atom. One excess electron into the carbon dots modifies their electronic transport properties and develops donor state. It has strong electron withdrawing ability and non-toxic environmental impact. Thus, N-doped carbon dots have been offered improved photoluminescence quantum yield and sensitivity against hazardous ions and chemicals [8,9]. In recent past, many researchers have taken benefits of N-doped carbon dots for photocatalysis, heavy metal sensing, bio sensing, LEDs, and other applications. It has been prepared using urea, diamines such as ethylene diamine, propane diamine, butane diamine or diethylene triamine as nitrogen source via microwave, hydrothermal, solvothermal and pyrolysis preparation methods [8–10]. The amines (NH-) are the prime group of molecular nitrogen and hold dual property of doping and passivation [8,11–13]. Previously, Manioudakis et al. found effectively improved green fluorescence from N-doped carbon dots using C6H8O7 and NH3 as carbon and nitrogen source respectively. They reported the impact of nitrogen doping on the photophysical properties of carbon dots [14]. According to the literature, N-doped carbon dots are able to exhibit blue and green fluorescence depending upon the inter or intra molecular H bonding of N molecule with surface of carbon atoms [16]. However, highly intense stable blue emission from N-doped carbon dots prepared by bio-based carbon source is most common [15–18]. While green emissions have also been reported by some researchers [19]. Green is most preferred color of visible spectrum, green dye or green fluorophores are used for biochemistry, nano-chemistry, and immunohistochemistry applications. Hence non-toxic, intense green fluorescent carbon dots with high quantum yield can be an alternative for green fluorophores for analytical applications.

Furthermore, a bio-derived plant-based synthesis of carbon dots including plant leaves, fruit juices, kitchen waste or other natural precursors as carbon source have drawn attention due to environment benign and sustainable materials processing. They can be prepared via various chemical routes like electrochemical oxidation, microwave irradiation and hydrothermal treatment [6]. Since, initial carbon precursor has a significant role in determining the stability and fluorescence of carbon dots, a good choice of initial precursor is more important. The acidic and basic nature of natural plant-based constituents influences the stability and fluorescence quantum yield. So far, many citrus fruits such as lemon, oranges, raw mango, grapes etc. and their peel-off were used as carbon source due to highly acidic nature [20–23]. All of them have demonstrated good optoelectronic properties and metal ion sensing, photocatalytic and energy applications. A recent study applied citrus acid and EDA as carbon and nitrogen initial precursor for synthesis of blue-fluorescent carbon dots and further used as fluorometric sensing for Fe3+ and Hg2+ dual ions [21]. The citrus fruits have rich amounts of citric acid, which forms citrazinic acid on reaction and promote the molecular fluorescence [22]. Earlier, Pooja devi et al. have used sweet lime peel waste to prepare carbon dots/TiO2 nanocomposite for photocatalytic applications [23]. In line with previous reported work, we used plant-based ingredient (freshly squeezed sweet lime juice) to develop a green-fluorescent carbon dots, which are further utilized as effective pH sensor. The photophysical properties such as UV–visible absorption, photoluminescence, Time Corelated Single Photon Count (TSPLC), Raman spectra were studied. Previously, Song et al. observed pH dependent fluorescence response in acidic medium in N-S doped carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal route using L-cysteine, ammonia. Further they demonstrated the high selectivity towards glutathione [24]. Jie et al. reported pH and Iodide sensitive fluorescence response from N-doped carbon dots derived from bacteria Listeria monocytogenes (LM) and urea by solvothermal method [25]. Hence most of the pH sensitive studies on carbon dots have been found on either acidic or alkaline medium [25–29] and the mechanism of both medium is rarely discussed. In this regard, a bio-based fluorescent carbon dots-based pH sensor for real samples analysis, fast monitoring, ease preparation, stable, and well explanation of mechanism are still needed.

It is difficult to find any published report using fresh sweet lime juice for fluorescent carbon dots and pH dependent fluorescent behavior. In this article, a simple and quick microwave carbonization of sweet lime juice in the presence of ethylenediamine is used to synthesize the nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs). Sweet lime is a citrus fruit, which is easily available, cheap, and bio-based ingredient. A systematic study on the N-doped carbon dots is carried out with different EDA amounts. Importantly, prepared N-doped carbon dots have shown pH dependent fluorescence response and applied as nano probe in pH sensing in real water samples with different pH. The mechanism is discussed and presented to gain a clearer vision to understand about it. It can be applied as pH nano probes for physiochemical and in-vivo biological pH sensing. The novelty of the work is to demonstrate the effective pH sensing ability, highly photo stable and anti-photo bleaching green fluorescence with high quantum yield (83 %) from N-doped carbon dots suspension. The non-toxic, bio-based initial ingredients, no additional dialysis equipped filtration requirement, and optimization of N-dopant concentration for luminescence properties make it different from other reported works. Further, the pH sensing results, selectivity of co-existence with metal ions, ionic strength, and real water samples analysis presented the reliable, easy, fluorescence sensing system, which can be sense in both (acidic and alkaline) medium. Moreover, improved thermal stability of prepared N-doped CDs has been checked up to 1000 °C. This has expanded the insight of using a straightforward and simple method to develop N-CDs from cheap natural bio-based product for pH sensing applications.

Sweet lime fruits were purchased from the local market. AR grade ethylenediamine (EDA) procured from Merck India was used as a surface stabilizer and for Nitrogen (N) doping as well. Double deionized (DI) water was used as solvent. Grade 1 Whatman and 0.22 μm syringe filters were applied for filtration. Carbon dots were prepared by microwave irradiation of sweet lime juice using 700 W Microwave Oven.

The synthesis method of carbon dots is quite similar to our previous report [30]. For the synthesis of carbon dots in greener way, fresh sweet lime juice was extracted from fruits and filtered juice was taken as initial carbon precursor. The 20 ml of fresh juice was mixed with an appropriate amount of EDA with w/v% (2.5, 5.0, 10, 12.5) and named as C1, C2, C3, and C4 respectively. The resultant solution was kept in microwave oven for 300 s. After microwave irradiation, the final brown colored product was cooled naturally up to room temperature. Further, the product was washed with deionized (DI) water and centrifugation was done to separate impurities, residual, and bigger particles formation. To get finest, pure carbon dots suspension, the product was further filtered with a nylon 0.22 μm syringe filter and resultant suspension is used for further characterization and measurements. The same procedure was followed for each sample. Scheme 1 illustrates the different stages of carbonization.

In addition, the prepared carbon dots presented good water solubility at room temperature. Sweet lime is rich in glucose, sucrose, fructose, and ascorbic acid which contains hydroxylic (-OH) and carbonyl (C=O) groups. These moieties are the reason for their better solubility in water and other solvents.

The response of N-doped carbon dots to pH was studied by taking 2 ml of carbon dots' solution at different pH. The pH values were regulated by adding appropriate amount from 1 mM solution of HNO3 or NaOH solution. The pH meter was first calibrated with AgCl reference electrolyte solution. Then pH values were varied from acid to base with addition of HNO3 & NaOH in calculated amount respectively. As mixed solution was stirred and allowed to react for 5 min. Thereafter fluorescence spectra of these carbon dots samples at different pH were taken in the range of 460 to 760 nm for an excitation of 440 nm. For pH measurement of real samples, water from RO(filtered water), pond & sea and apple cider, the samples were thoroughly filtered and sonicated. Firstly, the pH of these samples was measured by pH meter and then fluorescence spectra of carbon dots and real samples solution was recorded. With the help of pH dependent fluorescence spectra of carbon dots without real samples, and the response of fluorescence with real sample addition was analyzed and discussed.

Morphological study was carried out with TECNAI G20 HR-TEM 200KV Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The X'pert Pro Goniometer PW3050/65 wide angle X-ray diffractometer (XRD) operated at 40 kV and 30 mA with Cu-kα radiation (λ = 1.541 A) graphite monochromator (step size 0.04°) and integration time 1 s was used to record the diffraction pattern. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed with a monochromatic Mg Kα X-ray source (1253.6 eV) operating at 10 kV and 10 mA on a Shimadzu ESCA-3400 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). low energy Ar+ ions and electrons were used to develop charge neutralization in each test. The Bruker alpha FTIR spectrometer was used in the range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. Raman spectra of prepared carbon dots were recorded in Horiba JY- LABRAM HR Raman Spectrophotometer using 785 nm lasers in the range of 1800–1100 cm−1. Thermogravimetry analysis (TGA) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) measurements of samples were interpreted from the thermograms obtained from PerkinElmer STA 6000/8000 thermal analyzer. The prepared samples were tested under nitrogen atmosphere and heated up to 1000 °C at heating rate of 20 °C/min. Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Visible) absorption spectroscopy was performed on Agilent UV–Visible spectrophotometer in the range of 200 to 800 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra were taken using an Agilent spectrofluorometer. The fluorescence lifetime was measured by JOBIN-YVON Horiba TCSPC system.

The prepared N-doped carbon dots samples are examined by TEM. Fig. 1(a) and (b) depicts the TEM images of C2 and C4 samples respectively. It indicates the spherical shaped particles of prepared carbon dots. The histogram is shown in the inset of figures. From 1(a), C2 sample shows wide size distribution with most of the particles are in the range of 9-11 nm. Aggregation of small particles formed a cluster. While TEM image of C4 sample (Fig. 1(b)) indicates well dispersed spherical shaped particles. The calculated particle size is small as compared to C2 sample. The average particles size is about 7 nm. However, histogram plot indicating that most of particles below 7 nm also existed. This behavior shows the better passivation of carbon dots' surface by EDA amount.

Structural information of prepared N-doped carbon dots is studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The diffractogram pattern is presented in Fig. 1(c). A broad peak at around 19.90° indicates the highly disordered amorphous nature of carbon dots [31]. Murugan et al. also observed broad amorphous peak for pristine carbon dots functionalized with EDA at 19.18° [32]. Bhaisare et al. perceived single broad peak at 20° having mostly amorphous structure and extremely disordered carbon atoms [33]. However, with EDA incorporation, this peak is shifted from 19.90° to 19.78° along with the appearance of minor peak at 44.66°. This peak is assigned to (100) planes of standard graphene [13]. The shift of the prominent peak and emergence of new peak can be attributed to the strain induced by N dopant ions. It is supposed that with high amount of EDA, dopant N atom induced strain to the amorphous carbon atom, due to difference in their atomic radii. Previous studies also reported such disturbance created by passivation agent or doping material [34–38].

The interplanar spacing (d) is calculated using Bragg's law (Eq. (1)). Where λ represents the wavelength of Cu Kα radiation (1.54 Å), θ is the half of peak position angle in degrees and d is the interplanar spacing (nm). The calculated values of d are 0.445, 0.446 and 0.448 nm for C1, C2 and C3 samples respectively. Dong et al. synthesized carbon dots at three different temperatures (220, 250 & 280 °C) and all samples showed broad peak centered at 20.4° with interlayer spacing fairly similar to prepared carbon dots [15]. In addition, higher amount of EDA on carbon precursor most probably attached the amine group with surface of carbon dots via oxygen containing groups, thus imposed strain on the carbon dots. Further the presence of various moieties and their interactions are examined by FTIR and XPS spectra.
(1)
nλ = 2 dsinθ

Further, the effect of EDA on carbon dots is clearly reflected by the changes in transmission peaks given in Fig. 1(d). All samples exhibit peaks of OH stretching vibration, symmetric CH stretching vibration, amine I, amine III, CN bond, in-plane bending vibration of CH bonds, CC bond vibrations and CONH interaction vibrations. It indicates abundance of surface functional groups in the prepared samples. However, changes in transmission peak intensity and position are observed across the samples (Fig. S1 in Supplementary file). All the peaks with their assignments are tabulated in Table S1 in Supplementary file. The peak of OH stretching vibration becomes sharp and shifted from 3423 cm−1 to 3354 cm−1 for C1 to C4 sample respectively. It indicated increased interaction of amine groups with OH bonds with higher EDA content. Similarly, CH stretching bond at 2920 cm−1 in C1 is slightly shifted to 2363 cm−1 for C4 [39–41]. In addition, peaks between 1000 cm−1 to 1650 cm−1 show major changes in between the samples or with change in EDA amount in the samples. The peak at 1066 cm−1 represents vibration of amine I [42–45]. Notably, amine I bond vibration becomes sharper and more intense with increasing EDA content in the carbon dots (C1 to C4). This is due to the change in dipole moment around carbon atoms with interaction to N group. Emergence of a peak at 1237 cm−1 show vibration of CN bond and in-plane bending of CH bond in the prepared samples [13,46,47]. Further, the CC bond vibration peak around 1447 cm−1 loses its strength as well as blue shifted to 1400 cm−1 on EDA increment. While peaks of CONH bonds at 1641 cm−1 become intense and shift to 1617 cm−1. It is suggested that the weaking of CC bond and strengthening of CONH bonding is due to change in bond polarity [48–51]. The presence of N-pyridinic structure (C=N) is supported from the peak existence at 1624 cm−1. The stretching vibration of N-pyridinic structure is overlapped with CC vibrational [52].

Interaction with N-type of atom increases the polarity of bonds and hence probability of transition become high and intense peaks are observed [53,54]. Whereas all vibrational peaks in fingerprint region have shown shift towards lower wavenumber on higher EDA content. This might be due to change in bond length between the atoms which is also indicated by XRD analysis [55,56].

The surface states and elemental information are elucidated by XPS analysis. Fig. 2(a) represents the survey spectrum of C4 sample. From survey spectrum two peaks at 286.9 and 533.8 eV are corresponding to C1s and O1s signals, while a new peak of N1s at 401.8 eV along with C1s and O1s signals can be seen for N-doped carbon dots sample. The atomic percentage of C, O and N elements are 50.20, 44.32 and 5.48 respectively. Further, the high resolution C1s of N-doped carbon dots sample is fitted with two different peaks at 286.1 and 287.4 eV corresponding to C-N/C-O and C=N/C=O bonds respectively (Fig. 2(b)) [16,57]. Similarly, the high-resolution N1s peak is broad and centered at 401.9 eV (Fig. 2(c)). This binding energy is assigned to pyridine nitrogen (C–N–C, CN), indicating nitrogen bonded with the carbon atom [58–60]. The high-resolution O1s (Fig. 2(d)) contains two peaks at 532.7 and 534.2 eV selected to COOH/OH and N-C-O bonds respectively [61,62].

Every element has its own characteristic binding energy. The shift from these standard values is referred to chemical shift. Hence, chemical shift specifies the type of bonding and interaction of element with neighboring atoms. C1s electron have 284.6 eV of binding energy. In N-CDs, chemical shifts of 2.5 eV & 3.2 eV are recorded for CN and CN bonds respectively. It represents the different oxidation states and interaction of carbon atom with nitrogen atom. In the same way, chemical shift is obtained for N1s & O1s electrons of N-doped carbon dots sample. Usually, chemical shift of 1.5 & 2.8 eV in hydrocarbons corresponds to ether/alcohol and carbonyl groups attachment respectively. Hence, the elemental analysis suggested the dual work of EDA as surface passivator and as N-dopant. Previously, Martins et al. and Liu et al. observed this kind of behavior of N dopant in carbon dots [11,13]. Liu et al. has used EDA as nitrogen dopant and elemental analysis is quite in agreement with the reported carbon dots samples [13]. Similar analysis is reported by Dang et al. and Qu et al. for N-doped carbon dots [8,63]. Therefore, it can be stated that the EDA not only introduces nitrogen element in the carbon core but passivated the surface states also. Although, the weak peak of N1s shows low level of N doping in carbon dots for C4 sample.

A common and non-destructive method for characterizing both crystalline and amorphous carbon is Raman spectroscopy [48,64–66]. The ratio of sp 2 (graphite like) to sp 3 (diamond like) bonds strongly influences the physical features of carbon compounds. Disordered graphite exhibits two modes in its Raman spectra: the G peak at roughly 1580–1600 cm−1 and the D peak at around 1350 cm−1. These two peaks are attributed to zone center phonons of E2g symmetry and K-point phonons of A1g symmetry respectively [67,68]. Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectra of N-doped carbon dots with variation in N-doping. The broad and intense D band peak around 1353 cm−1 corresponds to sp 3 hybridize CC bond. Another peak around 1504 cm−1 can be seen in deconvoluted spectra represents D′ band of completely disordered sp 2 bonded amorphous carbon with distorted six-fold or other order rings [69]. Jawhari et al. reported that bands between 1500 and 1550 cm−1 start to appear due to amorphous nature of sp 2 hybridized CC bonded carbon atoms [70]. This mode is disabled in perfect graphite and only becomes active when disorder exists. The absorption region at 1589 cm−1 is attributed to G band which arises from in-plain vibration of E2g mode for disordered/amorphous carbon. However, an obvious decrease in scattering intensity of G band on N-doping is noticed. The change in intensity indicates the increase in surface disorder due to N doping by EDA molecules. Wu et al. reported mainly D band at 1360 cm−1 and G band at 1589 cm−1 in carbon quantum dots prepared from trinitro pyrene (TNP) as source for both nitrogen and carbon [46]. Further, the variation in intensity of G and D peaks elucidate about bond-angle disorder generated by EDA in carbon dots samples. To calculate the structural disorder present in the prepared samples, excess number of sp 3 to sp 2 bonded carbon atom is interpreted by I
D /I
G ratio. Where I
D and I
G are the intensities of the characteristic D & G band peaks respectively. I
D /I
G for C1 sample is 1.41 while for C4 sample, it is found to be 1.29 and rest other samples (C2 & C3) follow the same trend. The change in the ratio indicated the distortion created by EDA molecules in carbon dots samples confirmed to XRD and FTIR results [34,35,71]. Nguyen et al. analyzed Raman spectra of activated carbon and porous carbon dots prepared from hydrothermal treatment of banana peels. The presence of D band at 1360 cm−1 and G band at 1590 cm−1 with I
D /I
G ratio of ∼0.84 indicated lower graphitic phase or high amorphous carbon phase [72]. Therefore, the relatively high intensity of D band compared to G band states that the prepared samples of carbon dots are highly disordered and N doping tends to decrease the degree of crystallinity in the samples [73]. Hence in agreement with the XRD, Raman analysis also indicates amorphous nature of prepared carbon dots.

Thermogravimetry is an important analysis to study the thermal endurance of the N-doped carbon dots. The deformation of bonds with increase in temperature is reflected as weight loss. The thermograms for the C1, C2 & C3 samples up to 1000 °C in nitrogen atmosphere are presented in Fig. 4 . Total weight loss occurs in three stages. First stage weight loss from room temperature to 140 °C is generally ascribed to draining of moisture in samples. The second and major stage weight loss from 140 °C to 650 °C is found due to decomposition of covalent bonds in carbon core and at surface of carbon dots. The third stage weight loss >650 °C is due to growth of molecules. From Fig. 4, it is clear that the 2nd stage of degradation lasted longer for C2 and C3 samples than to C1 sample. After this stage, 22 %, 28 % & 32 % of weight remains for C1, C2 & C3 respectively. This clearly indicates an increase in thermal stability as consequence of N-doping in the N-CDs samples. The third stage (>650 °C) represents rearrangement of domains or recrystallization of carbon molecules in the samples. The study is in consistent with previous reports, in fact, even higher temperature response (up to 1000 °C) is also analyzed in our study [18,74–76]. Bandi and co-workers obtained two-step degradation for carbon dots up to 500 °C [4]. Li et al. studied increased thermal stability with increase in carbon dots content in TiO2 nanosheets [77]. Further, activation energy and other kinetic factors like entropy, enthalpy, and Gibb's free energy were calculated with help of derivative (DTG) and Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves, comprehensive analysis is presented in our recent published work [30]. The DSC plot is presented as Fig. S2 in Supplementary file. The weight loss stages are displayed by endothermic and exothermic peaks in DSC curve. We obtained a shift in the glass transition temperature and the temperature of decomposition in favor of the higher temperature side. This demonstrated that the thermal stability and melting temperature of carbon dot samples were enhanced by the development of a stronger link as a result of passivation and N-doping by EDA.

The absorption spectra of the N-CDs samples are shown in Fig. 5 . It is observed that the absorption spectra of all the samples are exhibiting a peak at 290 nm for n-π* orbital transition indicating presence of lone pair of C=O/C=N bond at the surface of carbon dots [78]. It presents that no change in the molecular state transition with higher amount of EDA. In addition, considerably lower optical density of C4 sample compared to others N-doped carbon dots indicated that change in the density of electrons energy states. Previously, Sahu et al. have found broad absorption at 288 nm for carbon dots synthesized from orange juice [79]. Karak and his research team observed a absorption peak at 283 nm in conjunction with a spectral tail extension into the visible range for carbon dots synthesized through the carbonization process of banana juice [80]. Hence, the absorption spectra of all prepared carbon dots samples showed high absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region with a tail extending into the visible region, making prepared carbon dots a potential candidate for optoelectronic, photovoltaic, and photocatalytic applications.

To find the optimal concentration, all the as prepared N-doped carbon dots suspension samples were examined under UV and visible light. Fig. 6 (a) & (b) shows the PL emission of N-doped carbon dots with different EDA content under UV chamber and day light respectively. One more sample (15 w/v%) named C5 is also tested for PL study to examine the intensity trend. As compared to others, a bright, highly contrast and green fluorescence is clearly seen for C4 sample under UV irradiation. Moreover, the impact of introducing nitrogen (N) atoms on the fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized N-doped carbon dots was investigated using visible light irradiation at 440 nm. From Fig. 6(c), all samples displayed a green emission band. As indicated by XPS analysis, EDA incorporated pyridine nitrogen oxide in the sample. It is believed that the pyridine type of nitrogen is responsible for green emission [10]. It develops new emitting states associated to pyridine-N resulting recombination through the radiative transition from emitting states. Thus, it validates the role of N in intensity and fluorescence of carbon dots. Importantly, C4 sample exhibits the most intense green emission at 530 nm relative to other N-doped carbon dots samples. The only 10 nm peak shift is observed between C1 and C4 samples. The narrow emission peak is suggested better passivation of carbon dots with 12.5 w/v% of EDA content (C4). All other parameters such as peak position, FWHM and intensity ratio of all samples are given in Table 1 .

There are two proposed models to describe the origin of fluorescence emission in carbon dots. The first is electronic transition from π to π* band involved in fluorescence and red shift in emission peak is originated by quantum size effect [81]. While the other model suggested the emission from surface defects of carbon dots and according to the surface state, the change in fluorescence is observed [82,83]. In this study, the origin of fluorescence is attributed to emission from surface defect states. Since EDA acts as surface stabilizer and source of N dopant, thus higher concentration of EDA passivates the surface defects by intramolecular H bonding interlinked between amine group and carboxyl group to the surface of carbon dots. Consequently, PL intensity is enhanced. Thus, the transition of electrons from N trap states to HOMO slightly shifts the wavelength of emission. At 12.5 w/v% of EDA content, more radiative electronic transitions are involved therefore emission is enhanced. As EDA content increases further, PL emission red shifted and intensity is quenched. It is the aggregation caused quenching in which higher concentration of the dopant ions aggregate the nanoparticles. Therefore, PL intensity is decreased, and emission is red shifted [84]. Further, the emitted color coordinates are checked by chromaticity diagram. The CIE 1931 plot for color coordinates of PL of prepared samples is given by Fig. 6(d). The CIE coordinates and color corelated temperature (CCT) for corresponding PL emission are given in Table 1. The CCTs are calculated with the help of Mc-Camy empirical formula given by Eq. (2) [85]. The calculated values are between 4700 and 5600 K.

A typical excitation dependent PL emission spectrum is observed for C4 sample. The peak position with excitation wavelength is given in Fig. 7(a). The red-shift in PL peak is supposed to be generation of new transition states between HOMO and LUMO levels by molecular states such as CO upon higher excitation wavelengths [86–88]. From the literature, it is believed that in carbon dots with amorphous carbon core electronic energy levels π and π* of the sp 2 spheres exist in the energy bandgap of σ and σ* energy states of the hydroxyl-bonded sp 3 matrix. Therefore, recombination of electron–hole pairs results in fluorescence emission. In addition, sharper PL peak on 440 and 480 nm excitation wavelength indicates the equivalent size radiative centers are dominating at these excitations. Notably under 440 nm excitation intense green emission (530 nm) is observed, while for 480 nm excitation a peak at 554 nm (yellowish green) is found (Fig. 7b). It shows that maximum pure green emission is found for 440 nm excitation only. Hence it is selected for further investigations for green-fluorescent carbon dots.

For clear evidence, the as prepared C4 sample was placed on the microscopic glass slides and photograph captured from fluorescent microscope under 455 nm excitation wavelength at 20 X magnification. The images without any filter (Fig. 7(c)) shows actual image of solution and with FITIC filter as shown in Fig. 7(d) is presented bright green emission. It shows the N-doped carbon dots is a luminescent center, which can be used for bioimaging applications. Previously, excitation dependent emission spectra from carbon dots were reported by many researchers [5,7,73–75]. Shen et al. observed 80 nm of red shift with excitation from 300 nm to 410 nm [51].

As prepared N-doped carbon dots (C4) sample is further analyzed for its fluorescence lifetime or excited state decay time by Time Corelated Single Photon Count (TCSPC). As depicted in Fig. 8 (a), the decay plot is well fitted to double exponential function given by Eq. (3) with R2 value 0.99846. The average lifetime of C4 sample was measured to be 3.47 ns. Previously lifetime in the range of 2 to 10 ns is reported for the fluorescence carbon dots with or without doping [89–91]. The short lifetime of prepared carbon dots sample indicates the emission is from the singlet excited state or the photoluminescence is fluorescence.

The relative quantum yield value is computed by using quinine sulfate (QY- 54 %) as standard for 350 nm excitation wavelength. The method is adopted from the previous literature [92] (mentioned in Supplementary file) comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance (<0.1) of the C4 sample suspension with standard value. The computed value of QY was to be 83 % for C4 sample under 350 nm excitation wavelength. High quantum yield is a good indication of prepared N-doped carbon dots for fluorescence applications. The photostability is another important factor to use fluorescent carbon dots for sensing applications. The photostability of N-CDs (C4) suspension is examined under continuous illumination for 6 h by 2000 lx tungsten lamp. From Fig. 8(b) (line A), prepared N-doped carbon dots (C4) are able to sustain 95 % fluorescence intensity up to 4 h. In the next hour, 10 % intensity degraded. Hence, the C4 sample sustained 75 % of the PL intensity even after 6 h of continuous lamp illumination. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of water-soluble N-doped carbon dots (C4) were further examined for a period of time in aqueous medium. It was observed that N-doped carbon dots hold >90 % of the PL intensity for a period of 5 days (Fig. 8(b), line B). Only 9 % degradation in intensity is found after 5 days. From day 5 to day 10, only 2 % PL intensity was quenched from 90 % to 88 %. The high photostability under visible light irradiation and over a time period is due to rich presence of carboxyl and amine groups present in the prepared N-doped carbon dots. It indicates highly stable long lasting green fluorescence from N-doped carbon dots sample can be suitable for sensing and bio-imaging applications.

To evaluate the ionic stability of the N-doped carbon dots, the PL emission spectra was recorded in the presence of different concentrations of sodium chloride (NaCl) and potassium chloride (KCl) solution up to 1 mol/L concentration. For this, an appropriate amount of salt was mixed with DI water then added to prepared N-doped carbon dots, sonicate it and record the PL emission spectra with 440 nm excitation wavelength. From Fig. 9 (a), it can be observed that by adding NaCl & KCl solution up to 1 M/L concentrations, the carbon dots sustained 91 % & 90 % of the PL intensity respectively. Moreover, only 4 % and 1 % of PL intensity enhancement is found for concentration change from 0.1 μM/L to 1 M/L for NaCl and KCl salts respectively. Although higher stability was observed for >1 mM concentration of both the salts. In addition, prepared N-doped carbon dots presented relatively higher stability for K+ ions. These results indicate that the prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibit high ionic stability for both Na+ and K+ ions.

A series of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots aqueous solutions were examined with pH ranging systematically from 1 to 11 at room temperature. A particular pH value is arranged with the help of 1 mM NaOH aqueous solution. The detailed procedure is given in the experimental section. The fluorescence intensity of all aqueous diluted samples in different pH values is monitored with a spectrofluorometer. The C4 sample (pH 4) is used for pH responsive PL characteristic curve. The acidic and alkaline environment has shown significant influence on fluorescence of N-doped carbon dots by protonation and deprotonation of surface moieties. The PL response curve with pH is depicted in Fig. 10 . The fluorescence emission wavelength is blue shifted from yellow to green (Fig. 10(a)) and fluorescence intensity increased with increasing pH from 1 to 5. The highest PL emission at 540 nm is found at pH = 5. Afterwards, it decreases drastically from pH = 5 to pH = 9, then slightly increases to pH 11. It is also observed that the fluorescence intensity is more quenched in strong alkaline environment (Fig. 10(b)). A possible reason for deterioration of intensity and red shift is the change in Fermi level of carbon dots core through modification in charge transfer process. The variation in pH impacts on the surface functional groups of carbon dots. Consequently, the electron-hole recombination process and PL emission is modified. The oxygen and amine functional groups are crucial for hydrophilicity, stability, and sensing applications. XPS and FTIR results confirmed the presence of amine group on surface and bonding of amine group with COOH and OH groups. Hence, it is supposed that in the acidic medium, due to protonation of amine functional groups (NH3 +) and electronegativity of N atom in -NH3 + could weaken the electron density in the carbon dots, resulting widening in energy band gap. Thus, blue shift in PL emission and increased PL intensity are found [93]. In contrast, in alkaline medium, deprotonation activates the -NH2 group. Meanwhile deprotonation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups could also occur. The formation of new surface states associated with functional groups consequently decreased optical energy band gap and non-radiative transition from these states quenched the fluorescence intensity. Scheme 2 represents the illustration of mechanism in acidic and alkaline medium. Such kind of behavior has been seen previously on pH sensing using graphene quantum dots and carbon quantum dots [1,94]. Furthermore, the UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples with different pH is given in Supplementary file (Fig. S3). No obvious shift in the optical density wavelength is found. However, intensity is modified with pH variation. It represents the no complex ground state formation [95]. A weak UV peak corresponding to 288 nm is obtained for pH 5. Hence the UV- visible absorption spectra also expressed the results in support with mechanism. The previous reports revealed the different results for finding the pH sensitive PL response in acidic and basic medium. Few of them found pH sensitive PL response either in acidic or alkaline medium [96]. Kurniawan et al. have found a decrease in PL intensity with red-shift in a strong alkaline medium of N-doped graphene quantum dots due to deprotonation of amine group [52]. Sahu et al. have reported lower PL intensity for more acidic solution. They found stable PL around 4.8 pH [79]. Jia et al. have observed a 90 % decrease in PL intensity due to electrostatic charging to carbon dots or shift in Fermi level by deprotonation with pH value 4 to 8 [97].

Furthermore, based on the previous reports, the N doping in carbon dots localizes the electrons in n orbital. The intensity of the UV absorption peak corresponding to 288 nm is affected by change in pH value (Supplementary file Fig. S3) indicating negative induction effect on the excitation. The protonation of the N-group in acidic medium weakens the negative induction effect of graphitic N [98]. Therefore, optical density is changed with pH of the solution.

A good linear relation between pH and PL intensity for pH ranging from 1 to 5 is found with R2 value 0.99 (as shown in Fig. 11a). It follows the linear fit of equation Y = 22.68 pH + 156.35 . It depicts that prepared N-doped CDs has potential as effective pH sensor for acidic medium. However, emission color wavelength and intensity tunablilty are found in both acidic and alkaline medium. Previous papers reported pH sensitive PL response mostly in acidic or alkaline nature of medium [99,100]. Assessing selectivity is a pivotal factor in appraising the efficacy of a fluorescent sensor. Hence, we investigated the impact of different metal ions on the PL intensity of N-doped carbon dots at pH = 5. The findings, as depicted in Fig. 11(b), demonstrate that metal ions exert only a marginal effect on the PL intensity compared to metal-free N-doped carbon dots. This underscores the high selectivity of N-doped carbon dots towards H+ ions only. Consequently, N-doped carbon dots exhibit substantial promise as proficient fluorescent sensors for monitoring pH fluctuations in the surrounding medium.

To test the reversibility of pH sensor, the pH of prepared N-doped carbon dots is changed from pH 3 to 9 in steps of 3, 5 and 9. The cycle is repeated four times. The recorded PL emission intensity is presented in Fig. 12 (a). The prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibited good reversibility of the pH sensor. Moreover, to check the applicability of prepared N-doped carbon dots samples as pH sensor. Different water samples such as sea water, RO water, pond water and apple cider of different pH value are taken, and real samples were tested with C4 sample in order to know their pH value. The pH of sea water, RO water, pond water and apple cider are measured by pH meter to be 8, 7, 7.7 and 2.7 respectively. To test the effectiveness of prepared N-doped carbon dots sample as pH sensor, real sample, and N-doped carbon dots sample (C4) is taken in 1:1 ratio and fluorescence intensity is recorded at 440 nm excitation. The results are given in Fig. 12(c). It is obvious from the figure that the maximum intensity for C4 sample is sustained at pH 5. While sea, pond and RO water having higher pH on alkaline side have decreased PL intensity along with slight red shift in PL emission wavelength. Apple cider being at pH 2.7, also degraded slightly in PL intensity and emission is at higher wavelength. The shift is clearly visible from CIE plot given by Fig. 12(b). It illustrates that the color of emission with real samples also changed from green to yellow with (x, y) coordinates. The observation is consistent with the laboratory examined results discussed above and a comparative study is shown by Fig. 12(d). Thus, the fluorescence study of prepared N-doped carbon dots with different pH indicates the effectiveness of prepared sample for a fast, reliable pH sensing system. The results shows the potential to be utilized as real time pH measurement or monitor system for biological, environmental, and physiochemical measurement applications.

Strong green-fluorescent N-doped carbon dots successfully prepared by microwave carbonization using fresh sweet lime juice and EDA as source of N dopant. Prepared N-doped carbon dots aqueous samples were examined by various characterization methods and results are discussed comprehensively. The morphological and structural studies represent spherical shaped <10 nm amorphous phase N-doped carbon dots. Further, XPS analysis suggested N-CDs are rich with nitrogen- and oxygen-containing surface functional groups with the presence of Pyridinic N group. Moreover, N-CDs presented good thermal stability up to 900 °C, thereafter the thermal stability and melting temperature were varied with N-doping. The prepared N-CDs presented highly photostable green fluorescence at 530 nm under UV and visible 440 nm irradiation with 83 % quantum yield. Further the fluorescent-based N doped carbon dots pH sensor exhibited good sensitivity over a wide range of pH in acidic and alkaline medium. The maximum PL intensity is obtained at pH 5. For below and above pH 5, PL intensity is decreasing continuously. The pH-responsive behavior is ascribed to the protonation-deprotonation of surface -NH group and shift in the fermi energy level of the carbon dots. Furthermore, the prepared N-doped carbon dots sample exhibited good reversibility between alkaline and acidic medium up to 4 cycles of pH change. Real water sample pH testing confirmed the ability of the N-CDs sample for pH sensing. Thus, it makes them valuable as fluorescent based pH sensor for future use in biological and bioanalytical applications.
